monteCarlo.title: Loss exceedance (Monte Carlo)
monteCarlo.empty: No simulation yet — run one to compute the portfolio loss distribution, VaR, and the loss exceedance curve.
monteCarlo.lecDesc: For each annual-loss threshold (x), the simulated probability the year's total losses are ≥ that threshold.
monteCarlo.breachNote: ≈{pct}% chance the year's losses exceed the {money} portfolio appetite.
monteCarlo.perRiskNote: {over} of {total} simulated risks carry a P90 loss above the {money} per-risk appetite cap.

correlations.title: Risk Correlation Matrix
correlations.clickHint: Click a cell in the upper triangle to edit a pair.
correlations.conceptHelp: How much two risks move together. Positive means they rise and fall together, amplifying tail losses; negative means one offsets the other. The Monte Carlo simulation samples correlated draws so portfolio VaR reflects this.
correlations.psdHelp: A valid correlation matrix must be positive semi-definite — otherwise the coefficients are mutually contradictory and the simulation cannot sample from them. Auto-suggest keeps it valid.

hierarchy.title: Risk Hierarchy
hierarchy.empty: No nodes yet. Add a node, then link risks to it (via the hierarchy API or the risk form).
hierarchy.conceptHelp: A branch of your org tree (business unit, geography, asset class). Attach risks to nodes and their ALE rolls up the tree, so you can see which part of the organisation carries the most quantified loss.

kri.title: Key Risk Indicators
kri.name: Name
kri.empty: No KRIs yet. Create one to track a leading indicator with RAG thresholds.
kri.conceptHelp: A measurable signal that a risk is worsening (e.g. failed-login rate). Set green/amber thresholds; a reading past them turns the KRI red. Direction says which way is bad — 'higher is worse' for most metrics, 'lower is worse' for e.g. patch coverage. A red KRI on a linked risk prompts re-assessment.

lossEvents.title: Loss events
lossEvents.intro: The system predicts losses everywhere — FAIR ALE, the simulated portfolio P50/P90, the loss-exceedance curve. This is where the world's answer comes back, so the forecasts can be scored.
lossEvents.emptyActuals: No actuals recorded yet — the forecasting stack is unfalsifiable until losses come back in. Record the next one below, or enable the finding / incident close-out integration to record them automatically.
lossEvents.whatHappenedPlaceholder: Customer breach response — settlement + vendor X
lossEvents.conceptHelp: A real loss that actually occurred (from a user entry, a finding, or an incident), with its date and amount. Recorded actuals are a scoreboard against the simulator’s predictions — they are not fed back into the FAIR model.

scenarios.title: Risk Scenarios
scenarios.intro: Create a what-if scenario, then simulate to compare its portfolio VaR against the live baseline. Overrides (field patches / synthetic risks) are authored via the scenarios API.
scenarios.name: Name
scenarios.conceptHelp: A set of per-risk overrides (e.g. a WAF cuts one risk's vulnerability from 0.6 to 0.1). The engine re-runs the Monte Carlo with the overrides applied and compares the resulting portfolio loss against the current baseline — the ROI of a control investment.

board.hygieneBody: {stale} of {total} risks carry a stale assessment ({pct}%) — overdue review, untouched past the age ceiling, or a signal that has moved since.

dash.quantEmpty: No risks quantified yet — open a risk and set SLE × ARO on the overview, or fill the FAIR inputs on the Quantification tab, to populate the loss-exceedance curve and top-by-ALE table here.
dash.sumLine: Σ of mean ALEs: {amount} — a sum of averages, not a distribution.
dash.sumTooltip: Summing each risk's mean ALE ignores correlation and tail compounding — bad years cluster. The simulated percentiles above are the portfolio's actual loss distribution: P50 is a typical year, P80/P95 are the years the board plans reserves for. The gap between the sum and these figures is real information, not noise.
dash.sumNudge: These figures are sums of averages. Run a simulation below to replace them with calibrated portfolio percentiles.
dash.coherenceAgree: Qualitative scores and loss estimates agree across the {count} quantified risks — no rank contradictions detected.
dash.coherenceDisagree: {count, plural, one {# risk} other {# risks}} where the matrix and the money disagree — one of the two assessments is probably wrong.
dash.coherenceBigger: score {score} vs {ale} — losses say this is bigger
dash.coherenceSmaller: score {score} vs {ale} — losses say this is smaller
dash.staleBody: {stale} of {total} risks carry an assessment the world may have moved past — overdue review, untouched for {days}+ days, or control tests newer than the residual.

aiGov.intro: One assessment, three readouts. Your answers map to OWASP AISVS, ISO/IEC 42001 and the EU AI Act at once. Answers save automatically — leave and return any time.
aiGov.criticalGapsSuffix: flagged (potential EU AI Act legal exposure). Review with your counsel.
aiGov.notApplicable: Not applicable to your AI system type ({conditional}) — counted as N/A.
aiGov.materializeNone: No new findings — your high-priority answers are covered.
aiGov.disclaimer: This is a self-assessment aid, NOT legal advice. EU AI Act risk classification and fundamental-rights determinations are legal calls owned by your counsel.
aiGov.attribution: Questions reference OWASP AISVS v1.0 (CC-BY-SA-4.0, © OWASP Foundation), ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (clause refs) and the EU AI Act (2024/1689, public domain).

sovereignty.intro: Six sovereignty dimensions — jurisdiction, residency, identity, exit-readiness, regulatory alignment and sovereign AI. Score each question from none (0) to leading (4); weak dimensions surface a suggested risk + control you can approve at the end. Nothing is saved until you create it.
sovereignty.weakSuffix: below the gap threshold. Review the suggested risks + controls below before creating them.
sovereignty.createError: Failed to create risks and controls.
sovereignty.materializeNone: Nothing new to create — your approved gaps already exist.
sovereignty.disclaimer: This is a self-assessment aid, NOT legal advice. EU sovereignty, residency and jurisdiction determinations are legal calls owned by your counsel.
sovereignty.attribution: The six-dimension model derives from the MIT-licensed Digital-Sovereignty-Assessment-Tool; regulatory material is clause-referenced, never reproduced.
